title: Sugar Crisis
tagline: TBD — pick from options in chat
description: A 12-year-old with no prior medical history is brought in by EMS after her mother found her unresponsive on the bathroom floor. She had been complaining of headaches, nausea, and excessive thirst for several days. This is new-onset DKA presenting at its most dangerous — with altered mental status signaling impending cerebral herniation.
visuals: Fruity acetone odor noted on breath

patient.history: No prior medical history. No known diabetes diagnosis. Mother reports 5-day history of polyuria, polydipsia, and fatigue progressing to nausea, vomiting, and diffuse abdominal pain over the past 24 hours. Patient was found this morning slumped on the bathroom floor, minimally responsive. No medications at home. No recent illness or injury. No family history of diabetes documented at this time.

emsReport: EMS was called by the patient's mother after finding 12-year-old Saoirse unresponsive on the bathroom floor. Paramedics established a 20-gauge peripheral IV in the right antecubital fossa en route and placed the patient on a non-rebreather mask at 15 L/min. No medications were administered in the field. Patient is being delivered to your bay with a decreased level of consciousness and deep, labored breathing.
learnMore: Pediatric DKA severity is classified as mild (pH 7.21–7.30, bicarb 11–15), moderate (pH 7.11–7.20, bicarb 6–10), or severe (pH <7.10, bicarb <5, or altered mental status). Cerebral edema is the leading cause of DKA-related death in children, occurring in up to 1% of pediatric DKA cases with a mortality rate of 21–24%. Early recognition using the Muir-Rosenbloom diagnostic criteria allows clinical diagnosis without neuroimaging — treatment should never be delayed for a CT scan.

phases.name: Triage
phases.narrative: Saoirse is wheeled into your bay on the EMS stretcher. She is a thin 12-year-old girl in obvious respiratory distress, exhibiting deep, rhythmic, sighing breaths. She opens her eyes to voice but does not track and mumbles only incomprehensible sounds to painful stimulation — GCS 9 (E3V2M4). Her skin is pale, dry, and cool to touch with tenting over her forearm. The odor of ketones is immediately perceptible from the doorway. EMS has placed her on the non-rebreather and established right antecubital IV access; the line is confirmed patent.

signs.label: Level of Consciousness
signs.finding: Opens eyes to voice, no tracking, mumbles incomprehensibly to pain. GCS 9 (E3V2M4).
signs.why:
Altered consciousness in DKA reflects the combined effects of severe acidosis and early cerebral edema.

- **Cerebral edema** in DKA causes neuronal compression due to osmotic fluid shifts and hypoperfusion-reperfusion injury
- **Severe acidosis** (pH <7.10) directly impairs neuronal membrane potential and mitochondrial function
- **GCS <14** is a major diagnostic criterion for DKA-related cerebral injury per Muir-Rosenbloom criteria
- A GCS of **9** places this patient at high risk for respiratory decompensation requiring airway protection
- Altered consciousness at presentation — before treatment begins — is a recognized presentation of severe DKA with cerebral injury

signs.label: Respiratory Pattern
signs.finding: Deep, regular, sighing respirations at rate of 32/min. Accessory muscle use absent. No stridor or wheeze.
signs.why:
This breathing pattern is classic Kussmaul respiration — the body's compensatory response to severe metabolic acidosis.

- **Kussmaul breathing** is a reflex response mediated by peripheral chemoreceptors detecting low blood pH
- Increased tidal volume and rate maximize **CO₂ elimination**, blunting the fall in pH
- In severe DKA, the **respiratory compensation** may generate a pCO₂ as low as 10–18 mmHg
- Failure of Kussmaul breathing (slowing rate, shallowing depth) signals **respiratory fatigue** — a pre-arrest sign
- Absence of stridor or wheeze rules out concurrent upper or lower airway obstruction as the cause

signs.label: Skin & Perfusion
signs.finding: Pale, dry skin. Skin tenting present on forearm. Capillary refill 4 seconds in all extremities. Extremities cool to the wrist bilaterally.
signs.why:
The compound skin finding reflects profound intravascular volume depletion driving reduced peripheral perfusion.

- **Skin tenting** reflects loss of interstitial fluid turgor — present in moderate-to-severe dehydration (estimated ≥7–10% body weight)
- **Cap refill >2 seconds** indicates peripheral vasoconstriction from volume-depleted shock physiology
- **Cool extremities** result from sympathetically mediated vasoconstriction redirecting blood to core organs
- In DKA, osmotic diuresis from glycosuria drives urinary free water losses of **5–10% body weight** over days
- **Pale, dry skin** and absent sweating are consistent with volume depletion rather than distributive (warm, flushed) shock

signs.label: Pupils
signs.finding: Pupils 5 mm bilaterally, equal and reactive to light, sluggish. No anisocoria.
signs.why:
Bilaterally equal, reactive pupils are a partially reassuring neurological sign in this context.

- **Equal pupils** argue against unilateral uncal herniation, which would produce a fixed, dilated, unilateral pupil (CN III compression)
- **Sluggish reaction** may reflect diffuse cerebral dysfunction from acidosis or early global cerebral edema
- Absence of **anisocoria** does not exclude cerebral edema — it only suggests herniation has not yet progressed to third nerve compression
- This finding should be monitored continuously; a change to **unequal pupils** would represent a herniation emergency

signs.label: Abdominal Exam
signs.finding: Abdomen soft, mildly diffusely tender to palpation. No guarding, no rigidity, no palpable masses. Bowel sounds hypoactive.
signs.why:
Mild diffuse abdominal pain is a well-recognized component of DKA and does not indicate a surgical emergency here.

- **Abdominal pain in DKA** is attributed to gastric ileus from hypokalemia, mesenteric ischemia from hypoperfusion, and direct irritant effects of ketonemia
- **Absence of guarding or rigidity** argues against peritonitis or a surgical abdomen
- **Hypoactive bowel sounds** are consistent with metabolic ileus secondary to hypokalemia and acidosis
- If pain persists or worsens after DKA correction, surgical causes (appendicitis, pancreatitis) must be reconsidered
- **Pancreatitis** may also be a DKA trigger or concurrent complication — lipase should be checked

signs.label: Breath Sounds
signs.finding: Clear and equal bilaterally. No crackles, no wheeze, no diminished zones. Trachea midline.
signs.why:
Equal, clear breath sounds bilaterally with a midline trachea are a reassuring finding in this patient.

- **Equal breath sounds** with a **midline trachea** effectively rule out tension pneumothorax as a contributor to the presentation
- **Absence of crackles** argues against pulmonary edema from aggressive fluid administration or aspiration
- **No wheeze** rules out bronchospasm as a cause of elevated respiratory rate
- This finding clarifies that the elevated RR and deep breathing are driven by **metabolic acidosis**, not pulmonary pathology
- Ongoing monitoring is warranted given the risk of **aspiration** in a patient with GCS 9 and active vomiting history

assessItems.label: HR 138 bpm
assessItems.why:
Tachycardia at 138 bpm is significantly elevated above the normal range of 60–100 bpm for a 12-year-old and warrants flagging.

- **Tachycardia** in this context is driven by hypovolemia from osmotic diuresis and vomiting, triggering compensatory sympathetic nervous system activation
- The heart rate increase attempts to maintain **cardiac output** (CO = HR × SV) as stroke volume falls with volume depletion
- HR >130 in a child this age with an altered level of consciousness signals **decompensating shock physiology**
- Persistent tachycardia despite initial fluid resuscitation should prompt reassessment for adequacy of rehydration

assessItems.label: RR 32/min
assessItems.why:
A respiratory rate of 32/min far exceeds the normal range of 12–20/min for a 12-year-old and reflects pathological hyperventilation.

- This is **Kussmaul respiration** — compensatory hyperventilation driven by metabolic acidosis detected at peripheral chemoreceptors
- The goal is to blow off **CO₂** to partially compensate for a severely low bicarbonate and blood pH
- Expected compensatory pCO₂ = 1.5 × [HCO₃] + 8 ± 2 (Winter's formula); in severe DKA with HCO₃ of 4, expected pCO₂ ≈ 14
- If the **actual pCO₂ is higher than expected**, a superimposed respiratory acidosis or respiratory failure is present
- Loss or slowing of Kussmaul breathing is a **pre-arrest warning sign** in DKA

assessItems.label: SBP 88 mmHg
assessItems.why:
A systolic blood pressure of 88 mmHg is below the acceptable range of 90–120 mmHg for a 12-year-old and indicates hypotensive shock.

- **Hypotension** at 88 mmHg in this age group signals that compensatory tachycardia and vasoconstriction are no longer maintaining perfusion pressure
- In pediatric DKA, hypotension indicates approximately **10% or greater** intravascular volume depletion
- This degree of hypotension places the patient at immediate risk for **end-organ ischemia**, including cerebral and renal hypoperfusion
- Isotonic fluid resuscitation (20 mL/kg NS bolus) is indicated emergently for hypotension in pediatric DKA

assessItems.label: SpO2 96%
assessItems.why:
SpO2 of 96% is within the normal acceptable range and is a reassuring finding in this patient.

- **SpO2 ≥95%** indicates adequate peripheral oxygen saturation on a non-rebreather mask
- This finding confirms the lungs are effectively oxygenating blood — the respiratory compromise is **ventilatory** (acid-base driven) not oxygenation-driven
- A patient with profound acidosis may have a **left-shifted oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve**, paradoxically showing preserved SpO2 despite reduced oxygen delivery
- SpO2 should be **trended continuously** — any drop below 92% would suggest aspiration, atelectasis, or airway compromise

assessItems.label: Temp 37.1°C
assessItems.why:
A temperature of 37.1°C is within the normal range and is reassuring against a febrile infectious trigger in this presentation.

- **Normal temperature** in new-onset DKA suggests the metabolic crisis is likely precipitated by **insulin deficiency** at initial diagnosis rather than an acute infectious illness
- DKA from infection classically produces fever; its absence does not rule out infection but reduces prior probability
- **Hypothermia** in DKA would be a concerning sign of septic shock or severe cardiovascular collapse
- An afebrile state in this context is one less variable driving the clinical instability

assessItems.label: Cap Refill 4 sec
assessItems.why:
Capillary refill of 4 seconds is significantly prolonged beyond the normal threshold of less than 2 seconds and signals impaired peripheral perfusion.

- **Prolonged cap refill** reflects peripheral vasoconstriction from sympathetic activation in hypovolemic shock
- Combined with SBP 88 and HR 138, this finding confirms **decompensated shock** with inadequate peripheral oxygen delivery
- Cap refill is a sensitive early indicator of **volume status** and perfusion in pediatric patients
- A capillary refill of **≥4 seconds** in a child has been correlated with greater than 10% dehydration in clinical studies

assessItems.label: Level of Consciousness
assessItems.why:
GCS 9 is critically abnormal and identifies this patient as meeting diagnostic criteria for DKA-related cerebral injury.

- **GCS <14** is a major criterion and GCS <12 meets a diagnostic criterion in the Muir-Rosenbloom cerebral edema scoring system
- A score of 9 indicates the patient cannot protect her airway reliably; GCS **≤8** is the traditional threshold for intubation consideration
- Altered consciousness at this level before treatment initiation suggests **pre-existing cerebral edema**, not a treatment complication
- This finding, combined with other clinical signs, mandates immediate neurology consultation and consideration of **hypertonic saline**

assessItems.label: Respiratory Pattern
assessItems.why:
Kussmaul respirations at a rate of 32/min represent a critically abnormal compensatory breathing pattern requiring immediate management.

- **Kussmaul pattern** (deep, regular, sighing breaths) is the clinical hallmark of severe metabolic acidosis with intact respiratory drive
- The pattern confirms the patient is currently generating significant **respiratory compensation** for the acidemia
- If this pattern deteriorates to shallow, irregular, or slowing breaths, it indicates **respiratory muscle fatigue** and impending arrest
- This finding is a diagnostic criterion for severe DKA and also a neurogenic pattern of concern for **cerebral edema** per Muir criteria

assessItems.label: Skin & Perfusion
assessItems.why:
The compound skin finding of pallor, tenting, 4-second cap refill, and cool extremities confirms severe dehydration with shock-level peripheral hypoperfusion.

- **Skin tenting** indicates loss of interstitial fluid, correlating with ≥7–10% body weight deficit in severe DKA
- **Cap refill 4 seconds** is the flagworthy element — it exceeds the normal threshold of <2 seconds and indicates vasoconstriction from compensated-to-decompensated shock
- **Pale cool extremities** reflect sympathetically mediated cutaneous vasoconstriction diverting blood to central organs
- Collectively these findings confirm the patient requires **immediate isotonic fluid resuscitation** (20 mL/kg NS bolus per current DKA guidelines)

assessItems.label: Pupils
assessItems.why:
Equal, reactive (though sluggish) pupils are a relatively reassuring neurological finding despite the severely altered mental status.

- **Absence of anisocoria** argues against unilateral uncal herniation compressing CN III, which would produce a fixed, dilated ipsilateral pupil
- **Equal bilaterally** suggests symmetric neurological dysfunction consistent with metabolic encephalopathy or diffuse cerebral edema rather than a focal lesion
- **Sluggish reaction** reflects global neuronal depression from acidosis and early cerebral edema but does not yet indicate a herniation syndrome
- This finding must be **serially reassessed** — any new anisocoria or loss of light reflex mandates immediate action

assessItems.label: Abdominal Exam
assessItems.why:
A soft abdomen without guarding or rigidity is a reassuring finding that argues against an acute surgical abdomen as the primary diagnosis.

- **No guarding or rigidity** effectively rules out peritonitis from perforation or severe mesenteric ischemia requiring emergent surgery
- **Diffuse mild tenderness** is expected in DKA from metabolic ileus, ketonemia, and mesenteric hypoperfusion
- **Hypoactive bowel sounds** are consistent with hypokalemia-induced ileus and do not represent a surgical emergency
- This finding allows the team to prioritize DKA resuscitation without diverting resources to surgical consultation at this time

assessItems.label: Breath Sounds
assessItems.why:
Equal, clear breath sounds bilaterally with a midline trachea are a reassuring finding that rules out several life-threatening respiratory differentials.

- **Equal bilateral breath sounds** with a **midline trachea** effectively exclude tension pneumothorax as a contributor to hypotension or respiratory compromise
- **Absence of crackles** argues against aspiration pneumonia or acute pulmonary edema
- **No wheeze** rules out reactive airway disease or bronchospasm as a cause of tachypnea
- This integrated finding confirms the elevated RR is **metabolically driven** (Kussmaul pattern) and not primarily pulmonary in origin

assessItems.label: Glucose
assessItems.why:
A glucose of 648 mg/dL is critically elevated and confirms severe hyperglycemia consistent with new-onset DKA.

- **Hyperglycemia** in DKA results from absolute insulin deficiency — without insulin, GLUT4-mediated cellular glucose uptake cannot occur
- The liver continues unopposed **gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis**, driving glucose further up
- Osmotic diuresis begins at glucose >180 mg/dL (renal threshold), causing the polyuria and polydipsia this patient experienced for 5 days
- A glucose of **648 mg/dL** generates a markedly elevated serum osmolality, contributing to both dehydration and CNS dysfunction
- Target glucose reduction rate is **50–100 mg/dL/hour** — faster reduction risks iatrogenic osmotic shift and worsening cerebral edema

assessItems.label: Venous pH
assessItems.why:
A venous pH of 6.98 is critically low and classifies this as severe DKA — the most dangerous category.

- **pH <7.10** defines severe DKA; pH <7.00 represents life-threatening acidemia with impairment of cardiac and neuronal membrane function
- The acidosis is primarily from **ketoacid accumulation** (beta-hydroxybutyrate and acetoacetate) from unopposed lipolysis and hepatic ketogenesis
- At pH <7.0, **cardiac contractility decreases**, arrhythmia threshold falls, and insulin receptor affinity is impaired
- Bicarbonate supplementation is **not recommended** in pediatric DKA — it can worsen cerebral edema by generating CO₂ that crosses the blood-brain barrier
- Treatment of pH is achieved by **insulin infusion** reversing ketogenesis, not by exogenous base

assessItems.label: HCO₃
assessItems.why:
A bicarbonate of 4 mEq/L is critically low and is internally consistent with the pH of 6.98, confirming severe metabolic acidosis.

- **Bicarbonate <5 mEq/L** meets the biochemical threshold for severe DKA (mild: 11–15; moderate: 6–10; severe: <5)
- Low bicarbonate directly elevates the **anion gap**: AG = Na – (Cl + HCO₃); with Na 128 and Cl 96, AG = 128 – (96 + 4) = 28 — markedly elevated
- The **anion gap elevation** is accounted for by circulating ketoacids replacing bicarbonate as buffer
- **Expected pCO₂** (Winter's formula): 1.5 × 4 + 8 ± 2 = 14 ± 2 mmHg; if measured pCO₂ is higher, a superimposed respiratory acidosis is present
- Rising bicarbonate during treatment is one of the earliest markers of **effective DKA resolution**

assessItems.label: Potassium
assessItems.why:
A serum potassium of 5.8 mEq/L is falsely reassuring and critically dangerous — it will drop precipitously once insulin is started.

- **Total body potassium is severely depleted** in DKA from urinary losses driven by osmotic diuresis and vomiting over days
- The serum K+ appears elevated because acidosis drives **extracellular potassium shift**: for every 0.1 unit decrease in pH, K+ rises ~0.6 mEq/L
- When insulin is administered, cellular K+ re-uptake resumes via **Na/K-ATPase activation** — serum K+ will fall rapidly, often to dangerously low levels
- Current guidelines require **K+ ≥3.5 mEq/L before starting insulin** — if K+ drops below 3.5 during treatment, insulin must be held and potassium replaced
- Hypokalemia post-insulin causes **fatal arrhythmias** including ventricular fibrillation

assessItems.label: Sodium
assessItems.why:
A measured sodium of 128 mEq/L appears low but must be corrected for hyperglycemia — the corrected sodium reveals true sodium status.

- **Pseudohyponatremia** in DKA: hyperglycemia draws water from intracellular to extracellular space, diluting measured sodium
- **Corrected Na** = measured Na + 0.016 × (glucose – 100) = 128 + 0.016 × 548 ≈ 128 + 8.8 = **137 mEq/L** — actually normal to low-normal
- A **falling corrected sodium** during DKA treatment is a warning sign for cerebral edema risk — it implies excess free water accumulation in the CNS
- Guidelines recommend monitoring corrected Na every 1–2 hours; it should **rise** as glucose falls during treatment
- A measured Na of 128 mEq/L with this degree of hyperglycemia is flagworthy for the pseudohyponatremia physiology it represents

assessItems.label: Beta-Hydroxybutyrate
assessItems.why:
A beta-hydroxybutyrate of 9.2 mmol/L is critically elevated and directly confirms the ketoacidosis driving this patient's severe acidemia.

- **Beta-hydroxybutyrate (BHB)** is the predominant ketoacid in DKA; acetoacetate and acetone are the other two ketone species
- Urine dipstick only detects **acetoacetate** — it may underestimate severity because BHB (the major species) is not measured by dipstick
- BHB >3.0 mmol/L confirms ketoacidosis; a level of **9.2 mmol/L** indicates extreme ketosis from absolute insulin deficiency
- During early treatment, BHB is oxidized back to acetoacetate, which **can make dipstick appear to worsen** while the patient is actually improving
- Falling BHB (trending toward <1.0 mmol/L) is the most reliable biochemical marker of **DKA resolution**

assessItems.label: Lactate
assessItems.why:
A lactate of 1.8 mmol/L falls within the normal range and is a reassuring finding in this critically ill child.

- **Normal lactate (less than 2 mmol/L)** indicates aerobic metabolism is preserved despite hypotension and shock physiology
- This finding helps confirm the anion gap is **ketoacid-driven** rather than from lactic acidosis
- A normal lactate makes concurrent **septic shock or severe mesenteric ischemia** less likely as contributors
- If lactate were greater than 4 mmol/L, it would indicate profound global tissue hypoxia and mandate evaluation for concurrent sepsis or hemorrhage
- This reassuring value does NOT negate the urgency — the ketoacid-driven pH of 6.98 is itself lethal if untreated

labs.name: Glucose
labs.why:
Critically elevated glucose from absolute insulin deficiency driving osmotic diuresis, dehydration, and CNS osmolar stress.

- **Insulin deficiency** prevents GLUT4-mediated glucose uptake in muscle and adipose
- Liver continues unopposed **gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis**
- Osmotic diuresis begins at renal threshold (~180 mg/dL), causing massive fluid and electrolyte loss
- Target reduction rate: **50–100 mg/dL/hour** to avoid rapid osmolar shifts

labs.name: Venous pH
labs.why:
Critically low pH from ketoacid accumulation — severe DKA defined as pH <7.10.

- **Beta-hydroxybutyrate and acetoacetate** accumulate from uninhibited hepatic ketogenesis
- At pH <7.0, **cardiac contractility** decreases and arrhythmia risk rises significantly
- Insulin reverses ketogenesis; **bicarbonate administration is not recommended** in pediatric DKA
- pH is the most direct measure of **acidosis severity and treatment response**

labs.name: HCO₃
labs.why:
Critically low bicarbonate confirms severe metabolic acidosis and is internally consistent with pH 6.98.

- **Bicarbonate <5 mEq/L** is the biochemical threshold for severe DKA
- Low HCO₃ generates a **markedly elevated anion gap** (AG = Na – Cl – HCO₃ = 28)
- Bicarb should **rise with insulin treatment** as ketogenesis is reversed
- Winter's formula predicts compensatory pCO₂ = 1.5 × HCO₃ + 8 ± 2 = **14 ± 2 mmHg**

labs.name: Potassium
labs.why:
Critically elevated but deceptive — reflects transcellular shift from acidosis; total body stores are severely depleted.

- Each 0.1 pH unit decrease shifts approximately **0.6 mEq/L K⁺ extracellularly**
- Total body K⁺ is depleted from urinary losses over days of osmotic diuresis
- Insulin administration will drive K⁺ back intracellularly via **Na/K-ATPase**, causing rapid fall
- Potassium must be **≥3.5 mEq/L before starting insulin**; K⁺ replacement must run concurrently

labs.name: Sodium
labs.why:
Low measured sodium represents pseudohyponatremia from osmotic dilution by hyperglycemia; corrected Na is ~137 mEq/L.

- **Corrected Na** = 128 + 0.016 × (648 – 100) = **~137 mEq/L** — within acceptable range
- A **falling corrected sodium** during treatment warns of free water accumulation and cerebral edema risk
- Corrected Na should **rise as glucose falls** — failure to rise is an early cerebral edema warning
- Monitoring corrected Na every 1–2 hours is a core DKA management task

labs.name: Beta-Hydroxybutyrate
labs.why:
Critically elevated BHB directly confirms severe ketoacidosis as the driver of this patient's pH of 6.98.

- **BHB >3.0 mmol/L** confirms DKA; 9.2 mmol/L indicates extreme ketosis
- BHB is the **predominant ketoacid** — not detected by urine dipstick (which detects acetoacetate)
- As treatment begins, BHB oxidizes to acetoacetate, potentially **worsening dipstick appearance** while patient improves
- Target BHB <1.0 mmol/L for biochemical DKA resolution

labs.name: Lactate
labs.why:
Normal lactate level is a reassuring finding that helps characterize the anion gap as ketoacid-driven rather than lactate-driven.

- **Lactate less than 2.0 mmol/L** indicates aerobic metabolism is still occurring despite hypotension
- Helps exclude **septic shock** and severe mesenteric ischemia as primary contributors to acidosis
- Anion gap elevation is accounted for by **circulating ketoacids**, not lactate accumulation
- A rising lactate during resuscitation would trigger reassessment for concurrent infection or hypoperfusion

phases.name: Escalation
phases.narrative: Despite EMS oxygen via non-rebreather, Saoirse's GCS remains at 9. Her Kussmaul respirations continue at 32/min and she does not respond to verbal commands. Blood pressure has not improved from 88/58 mmHg, and peripheral perfusion remains grossly impaired at 4-second cap refill. The glucometer confirms a critical glucose of 648 mg/dL. Labs confirm severe DKA: pH 6.98, HCO₃ 4, BHB 9.2 mmol/L, and a serum potassium of 5.8 mEq/L — elevated from transcellular shift but with depleted total body stores. The right antecubital IV line established by EMS is patent and ready to use. The PICU team is being activated and the pediatric endocrinologist is on the phone. Breath sounds are clear and equal bilaterally with a midline trachea; there is no evidence of pneumothorax. You must now act — this child's brain is in immediate jeopardy.

signs.label: GCS Trending
signs.finding: GCS now 8 (E2V2M4) — eye opening now requires painful stimulus only. Response to voice lost since initial assessment.
signs.why:
A declining GCS in the context of severe DKA signals progressive cerebral edema with increasing intracranial pressure.

- **GCS declining from 9 to 8** crosses the traditional intubation threshold (GCS ≤8) and signals airway protection is urgently needed
- Dropping eye-opening response indicates **progressive cortical depression** from rising ICP and cerebral ischemia
- Per Muir-Rosenbloom criteria, declining GCS satisfies **major diagnostic criteria** for DKA-related cerebral injury
- This progression mandates immediate **hypertonic saline or mannitol** and elevation of the head of bed to 30°

signs.label: Posturing Response
signs.finding: Sternal rub elicits bilateral upper extremity flexion and wrist extension. Lower extremities extend. Consistent with decorticate posturing.
signs.why:
Decorticate posturing is a diagnostic criterion for DKA-related cerebral injury and signals critical ICP elevation.

- **Decorticate posturing** (flexion of arms, extension of legs) indicates dysfunction at or above the midbrain-diencephalon junction
- Per Muir-Rosenbloom criteria, **abnormal motor response to pain** meets a diagnostic criterion for cerebral edema — the highest-weight category
- This finding, combined with GCS ≤8, removes all diagnostic uncertainty: cerebral edema treatment must begin immediately
- Progression to **decerebrate posturing** (extension of all four limbs) would indicate brainstem involvement and impending herniation

signs.label: Respiratory Effort
signs.finding: Breathing rate decreasing to 24/min, depth shallowing. Occasional pauses noted. Accessory muscle use now visible.
signs.why:
Slowing and shallowing of previously vigorous Kussmaul respirations indicates respiratory muscle fatigue and impending respiratory arrest.

- **Loss of Kussmaul pattern** is an ominous sign — it means respiratory compensation for acidemia is failing
- As respiratory drive slows, **pCO₂ rises**, worsening the acidosis and accelerating ICP elevation (CO₂ is a potent cerebral vasodilator)
- **Apneic pauses** in the context of declining GCS indicate brainstem respiratory center dysfunction from herniation
- BVM ventilation staged and airway protection is now a time-critical priority

signs.label: Blood Pressure Trend
signs.finding: Repeat BP 84/52 mmHg. Peripheral pulses weakening. Femoral pulse still palpable and strong. Radial pulse diminished.
signs.why:
Declining blood pressure with weakening peripheral pulses confirms worsening hypovolemic shock requiring immediate fluid resuscitation.

- **BP 84/52** represents further deterioration from 88/58 at triage, indicating ongoing volume depletion outpacing compensatory mechanisms
- **Palpable femoral but diminished radial pulse** is a clinical sign of progressive shock — central pulses are maintained longer than peripheral
- In pediatric DKA, hypotension indicates **≥10% body weight fluid deficit** and mandates isotonic resuscitation
- Caution: fluid must be given in controlled 20 mL/kg aliquots — rapid overcorrection increases cerebral edema risk

signs.label: Pupils (Repeat)
signs.finding: Left pupil now 6 mm, right pupil 4 mm. Left pupil sluggish, right pupil briskly reactive. New anisocoria noted.
signs.why:
New anisocoria with left pupil dilation represents CN III compression from uncal herniation — this is a neurological emergency.

- **Left pupil dilation** with sluggish response indicates compression of the left oculomotor nerve (CN III) by the uncus as it herniates over the tentorium
- **Anisocoria** in this clinical context is a herniation syndrome until proven otherwise — not a pre-existing condition
- This finding elevates the neurological emergency to **imminent transtentorial herniation** requiring immediate hyperosmolar therapy
- Hypertonic saline (3% NaCl, 3–5 mL/kg) or mannitol must be administered **within minutes** of recognizing this sign

defib.label: Attach Defibrillator / Cardiac Monitor
defib.fb:
Continuous cardiac monitoring is now active. ECG shows sinus tachycardia with peaked T-waves consistent with the borderline hyperkalemia. No dysrhythmia detected at this time. The monitor will provide critical early warning if potassium shifts cause ST changes or QRS widening as insulin therapy begins.

- **Peaked T-waves** are the earliest ECG change of hyperkalemia, seen when K⁺ exceeds 5.5 mEq/L
- **QRS widening** and **sine wave pattern** are later, pre-arrest changes requiring immediate calcium, insulin, and bicarb
- Continuous monitoring is mandatory during DKA management due to rapid potassium flux once insulin begins

bvmReady.label: Bring Bag-Mask to Bedside
bvmReady.fb:
Bag-mask ventilation equipment has been brought to the bedside and is staged for immediate use. With GCS now 8 and respirations slowing, this was the correct preparatory action — the team must be ready to ventilate immediately if respiratory effort ceases.

- **GCS ≤8** with declining respiratory effort meets criteria for **airway intervention readiness**
- Staging the BVM now means **zero delay** if apnea occurs in the next minutes
- If ventilation is required, target a rate that maintains **pCO₂ ≈ 35 mmHg** — do NOT hyperventilate, which reduces pCO₂ too low and causes cerebral vasoconstriction

bvm.label: Begin Bag-Mask Ventilation
bvm.fb:
Bag-mask ventilation is not yet indicated — Saoirse still has spontaneous respiratory effort, albeit slowing. Beginning BVM now would inappropriately take over the patient's drive before respiratory failure is established and risks inflating the stomach, increasing aspiration risk in a patient who has been vomiting.

- **BVM is indicated** when spontaneous respiratory effort is absent, ineffective, or pCO₂ is critically rising
- **Premature BVM** may suppress the patient's compensatory Kussmaul drive and worsen CO₂ retention
- The BVM should be **staged at bedside** (bvmReady) — intervene with active ventilation only at apnea or GCS ≤8 with loss of effort

glucometer.label: Confirm Bedside Glucose
glucometer.fb:
Bedside glucometer reads HI — glucose exceeds the device's upper limit of 500 mg/dL. Lab-confirmed glucose is 648 mg/dL. This confirms critical hyperglycemia consistent with new-onset DKA. The glucometer result corroborates lab data and guides timing of dextrose addition to IV fluids once glucose approaches 250–300 mg/dL during insulin therapy.

- **Glucometer HI result** in a DKA patient confirms glucose >500 mg/dL — always confirm with lab value
- Dextrose should be **added to IV fluids** when glucose falls below 250–300 mg/dL to prevent hypoglycemia while insulin continues
- Serial glucose checks every **1–2 hours** are required during DKA management

pupilCheck.label: Perform Pupil Assessment
pupilCheck.fb:
Critical finding: Left pupil is now 6 mm and sluggish; right remains 4 mm and briskly reactive. New anisocoria has developed since triage, indicating CN III compression from uncal herniation. This is the most urgent finding on this screen — it demands immediate hyperosmolar therapy.

- **Anisocoria** from a fixed or sluggish dilated pupil signals **uncal herniation** compressing CN III
- This is a **diagnostic criterion** in the Muir-Rosenbloom cerebral edema scoring system (cranial nerve palsy)
- Hyperosmolar therapy (3% saline or mannitol) must be given **within minutes** of this finding — do not wait for imaging

o2mask.label: Apply High-Flow O₂ via Non-Rebreather
o2mask.fb:
The patient arrived on a non-rebreather mask placed by EMS — supplemental oxygen is already in place. Selecting this tool again does not provide additional clinical benefit at this time. Direct your attention to the neurological emergency and hemodynamic instability.

- **EMS already established** NRB mask at 15 L/min en route — documented in the EMS report
- Oxygen saturation is 96% — oxygenation is not the primary problem; the crisis is **cerebral edema and hypovolemia**
- Prioritize **hyperosmolar therapy** and **fluid resuscitation** over airway maneuvers that are already in place

ivKit.label: Confirm IV Patency / Establish Second Access
ivKit.fb:
The right antecubital 20-gauge IV placed by EMS is confirmed patent — brisk blood return obtained and flushed without resistance. A second large-bore IV has been placed in the left antecubital fossa. Two-line access is now established, allowing simultaneous infusion of hyperosmolar therapy and isotonic resuscitation without line competition.

- **Confirming existing IV patency** is critical before administering time-sensitive medications
- **Second IV access** allows simultaneous administration of fluid bolus and 3% saline or other medications
- A 20-gauge peripheral IV is adequate for DKA management unless the patient requires **IO access** for cardiac arrest or failed IV attempts

hypertonic.label: 3% Hypertonic Saline — 5 mL/kg IV over 30 min
hypertonic.fb:
Hypertonic saline 3% at 5 mL/kg (210 mL for 42 kg) is administered over 30 minutes. This is the correct first-line treatment for DKA-related cerebral edema. By rapidly raising serum osmolality, it creates an osmotic gradient that draws free water out of swollen cerebral neurons and back into the vascular compartment, reducing ICP.

- **3% NaCl** raises serum osmolality rapidly — each mEq rise in sodium reduces cerebral water by ~2 mL/kg
- Dose: **3–5 mL/kg over 30 minutes** per current pediatric DKA cerebral edema guidelines (ISPAD 2022, BSPED 2021)
- **Hypertonic saline is now preferred over mannitol** by many centers due to volume expansion benefit in hypovolemic DKA patients
- IV fluids should be **reduced to ½ maintenance rate** concurrently with hyperosmolar therapy
- Clinical response (improving GCS, resolving anisocoria) typically begins within **15–30 minutes**

mannitolMed.label: Mannitol — 0.5–1 g/kg IV over 20 min
mannitolMed.fb:
Mannitol 0.5 g/kg (21 g; 105 mL of 20% solution for 42 kg) would be an acceptable alternative to 3% saline for treatment of DKA-related cerebral edema. However, in a patient who is already hypotensive and volume-depleted, mannitol's osmotic diuretic effect will worsen dehydration — making hypertonic saline the preferred agent in this specific patient.

- **Mannitol** reduces ICP via osmotic mechanism: raises serum osmolality, draws fluid from brain into vasculature
- However, mannitol is an **osmotic diuretic** — it will increase urine output and worsen the existing volume depletion
- **3% saline is preferred** in hypotensive DKA patients because it treats ICP without exacerbating hypovolemia
- Both agents have comparable efficacy; the key point is **not to delay treatment** while choosing between them

nsBolus.label: Normal Saline — 20 mL/kg IV Bolus
nsBolus.fb:
Normal saline at 20 mL/kg (840 mL) is infused over 30 minutes via the confirmed IV access. This isotonic resuscitation bolus directly addresses the hypotensive hypovolemic shock from days of osmotic diuresis. Controlled isotonic resuscitation in 20 mL/kg aliquots is recommended — avoid boluses exceeding 20 mL/kg without reassessment, as rapid overcorrection increases osmolar flux and cerebral edema risk.

- **20 mL/kg NS bolus** is the standard initial resuscitation for hypotensive or severely dehydrated pediatric DKA
- Reassess hemodynamics after each bolus — the goal is **restoring perfusion pressure**, not rapid full-volume replacement
- **Boluses >50 mL/kg** in the first 4 hours have been associated with increased cerebral edema risk
- After hemodynamic stabilization, switch to **controlled deficit replacement** over 48 hours per DKA fluid protocols

dextrose.label: Dextrose 10% — Add to Maintenance Fluids
dextrose.fb:
Adding dextrose to IV fluids is not indicated at this time — glucose is critically elevated at 648 mg/dL. Administering glucose now would worsen hyperglycemia, increase osmolality, and potentially accelerate cerebral edema. Dextrose should be added to IV fluids only when glucose falls to 250–300 mg/dL during insulin therapy.

- **Dextrose is contraindicated** when glucose is >250 mg/dL during DKA management
- Dextrose addition prevents hypoglycemia when insulin therapy lowers glucose to safe levels
- Target for dextrose addition: glucose **250–300 mg/dL**, typically 4–6 hours into insulin infusion
- **Never give dextrose** as a bolus (D50) in DKA — it will cause dangerous osmolar spikes and worsen cerebral edema

lorazepam.label: Lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg IV — Seizure Treatment
lorazepam.fb:
Lorazepam is not indicated at this time — Saoirse is not seizing. The abnormal posturing observed is decorticate posturing from cerebral edema with elevated ICP, not tonic-clonic seizure activity. Administering lorazepam would suppress respiratory drive in a patient already at risk for respiratory failure without addressing the underlying cause.

- **Decorticate posturing ≠ seizure** — posturing reflects brainstem compression, not ictal activity
- Benzodiazepines **suppress respiratory drive** — dangerous in a patient with GCS 8 and declining respiratory effort
- If a **true seizure** were observed (rhythmic limb movements, nystagmus, autonomic instability), lorazepam would be appropriate
- The correct treatment for posturing from cerebral edema is **hyperosmolar therapy** (3% saline or mannitol), not anticonvulsants

acetaminophen.label: Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg IV/PR
acetaminophen.fb:
Acetaminophen is not indicated — the patient is afebrile at 37.1°C and pain management is not a current priority in this life-threatening emergency. Administering acetaminophen consumes resources and nursing attention without addressing cerebral edema, hypotension, or acidosis.

- **Temperature 37.1°C** is within normal range — antipyretic therapy has no indication
- In a hemodynamically unstable patient with cerebral herniation, **pain management is not the priority**
- Acetaminophen would not address any of the active life-threatening problems in this scenario
- This is a classic distractor — it appears as a reasonable medication but is wrong in this context

reassessment.narrative: Fifteen minutes after hypertonic saline infusion began, Saoirse's left pupil has returned to 4 mm and is now briskly reactive — anisocoria has resolved. GCS has improved from 8 to 11 (E3V3M5), with eye opening returning to voice and purposeful withdrawal to pain. Decorticate posturing is no longer elicitable. Blood pressure has improved to 96/62 mmHg following the NS bolus, and peripheral pulses are now palpable in the radial position bilaterally. Respiratory effort has restabilized at 26/min with continued deep Kussmaul pattern and improved depth of breath. Bedside glucose remains elevated at 580 mg/dL — appropriate for this stage. The patient remains critically ill with severe DKA, but the immediate herniation crisis has been interrupted.

signs.label: GCS Improved
signs.finding: GCS 11 (E3V3M5). Eye opening to voice restored. Purposeful withdrawal to pain. No posturing.

signs.label: Pupils Equal
signs.finding: Pupils 4 mm bilaterally, equal and briskly reactive. Anisocoria fully resolved.

signs.label: Hemodynamics Improved
signs.finding: BP 96/62 mmHg. Radial pulses now palpable bilaterally. Cap refill 3 seconds.

signs.label: Respiratory Stabilizing
signs.finding: Rate 26/min, deep Kussmaul pattern maintained. No apneic pauses. Effort improving.

stabilizationSummary: Hypertonic saline (3% NaCl, 5 mL/kg) reversed the osmotic gradient driving cerebral edema, resolving anisocoria and improving GCS from 8 to 11 by drawing excess free water out of swollen cerebral tissue. The 20 mL/kg normal saline bolus restored intravascular volume, improving blood pressure from 88/58 to 96/62 mmHg and restoring palpable radial pulses. Cardiac monitoring confirmed peaked T-waves from borderline hyperkalemia with no dysrhythmia, and confirmed IV access established two-line readiness for the insulin infusion and continued controlled fluid resuscitation that will now guide definitive DKA management over the next 48 hours.

debrief.summary: Saoirse presented in severe new-onset DKA complicated by cerebral edema with early uncal herniation — one of the most time-critical emergencies in pediatric critical care. The scenario demanded simultaneous recognition of hypovolemic shock requiring fluid resuscitation AND cerebral edema requiring hyperosmolar therapy, with careful attention to not over-resuscitate a patient whose brain was already under osmotic stress. The key teaching points: cerebral edema is a clinical diagnosis requiring no imaging before treatment; hypertonic saline is the preferred hyperosmolar agent in hypotensive DKA patients; potassium must be monitored before and during insulin initiation; and fluid replacement must be measured and controlled — not rapid.

explainers.title: DKA Cerebral Edema: Recognize and Treat Before You Scan
explainers.content:
DKA-related cerebral injury is the leading cause of death in pediatric DKA and is a clinical diagnosis.

- **Muir-Rosenbloom criteria**: 1 diagnostic criterion, OR 2 major criteria, OR 1 major + 2 minor = 92% sensitivity
- **Diagnostic criteria**: abnormal motor response to pain, decorticate/decerebrate posturing, cranial nerve palsy, abnormal neurogenic respirations
- **Major criteria**: altered mental status, declining GCS, sustained HR deceleration, age-inappropriate incontinence
- **Minor criteria**: vomiting, headache, lethargy, diastolic BP >90 mmHg, age <5 years
- **Do NOT delay treatment for CT** — imaging is not required and herniation will progress during transport
- **3% NaCl at 3–5 mL/kg** over 30 minutes is preferred in hypotensive DKA over mannitol (which worsens dehydration)
- **Head of bed elevated to 30°** reduces ICP by promoting cerebral venous drainage
explainers.tldr: Cerebral edema in DKA is a clinical diagnosis. Treat with 3% saline NOW — don't wait for imaging.

explainers.title: The Potassium Trap in DKA: Why a High K⁺ is Actually an Emergency
explainers.content:
Serum potassium in DKA is almost always falsely elevated — the real danger is what happens when insulin starts.

- **Total body K⁺ is severely depleted** from days of osmotic diuresis (urinary K⁺ wasting)
- **Acidosis shifts K⁺ extracellularly**: for every 0.1 unit drop in pH, serum K⁺ rises ~0.6 mEq/L
- A K⁺ of 5.8 at pH 6.98 may represent a true whole-body K⁺ deficit of **3–5 mEq/kg**
- When insulin begins, Na/K-ATPase is reactivated and K⁺ moves rapidly back intracellularly
- **Insulin must not start until K⁺ ≥3.5 mEq/L** — hypokalemia during insulin infusion causes fatal ventricular arrhythmias
- **ECG monitoring** for peaked T-waves (hyperkalemia) and QT prolongation (hypokalemia) is mandatory throughout DKA resuscitation
- Potassium replacement should begin at a rate of **0.1–0.3 mEq/kg/hour** added to maintenance fluids once K⁺ is confirmed safe
explainers.tldr: High serum K⁺ in DKA is a trap — total body stores are empty. Monitor and replace before and during insulin.

explainers.title: Fluid Strategy in Severe DKA: Resuscitate Without Drowning the Brain
explainers.content:
Fluid management in severe pediatric DKA requires balancing two opposing risks: hemodynamic collapse from volume depletion versus cerebral edema from osmolar overcorrection.

- **Initial bolus**: 20 mL/kg isotonic NS over 30 min for hypotension or severe dehydration — repeat once if needed
- **Avoid boluses >50 mL/kg** in the first 4 hours — associated with significantly increased cerebral edema risk
- After hemodynamic stabilization, replace total deficit (estimated at 7–10% body weight in severe DKA) **evenly over 48 hours**
- **Monitor corrected sodium hourly**: corrected Na = measured Na + 0.016 × (glucose – 100); it should rise as glucose falls
- **A falling corrected sodium** during treatment is an early warning sign of free water accumulation and cerebral edema risk
- **Dextrose** should be added to IV fluids when glucose reaches 250–300 mg/dL to allow insulin to continue clearing ketones without causing hypoglycemia
- **Bicarbonate is contraindicated** — it generates CO₂ that crosses the blood-brain barrier and may worsen cerebral edema while paradoxically worsening intracellular acidosis
explainers.tldr: Resuscitate with controlled 20 mL/kg aliquots. Monitor corrected sodium hourly. Replace deficit over 48 hours — never rush.
